notice: Macro Regime Scanner v0.24 public-source data contract. Treasury, CFTC COT, EIA energy, USDA/NASS agriculture, and BLS inflation/labor lanes are live/workflow-ready. Price-derived lanes remain excluded.

assets.name: U.S. Dollar Fundamental Pressure
assets.coverage: public-source prototype | EIA energy fundamentals | BLS inflation/labor
assets.mainConflict: Inflation and labor must be read with Treasury pressure and positioning
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for U.S. Dollar Index: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher relative policy rates usually support USD; lower or dovish policy usually pressures it.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider yield spreads in favor of USD usually support the currency pair; narrowing spreads usually pressure it.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider inflation-adjusted yield advantage usually supports USD; a weaker real-yield spread usually pressures it.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect USD through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Core PCE is important for policy expectations; hotter PCE usually supports rate/yield pressure and can support the dollar side of USD.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger relative growth can support USD by attracting capital and lifting rate expectations.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer data can support USD if it lifts growth and rate expectations.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect USD through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Tighter conditions can support safe-haven currency demand and pressure high-beta FX linked to USD.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for USD. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on USD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels. | U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: EIA energy tightness can affect U.S. Dollar Fundamental Pressure through inflation pressure, rate expectations, and risk appetite. It is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger U.S. inflation can support the dollar if it raises Fed-policy and yield pressure; softer inflation can reduce that support.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger U.S. inflation can support the dollar if it raises Fed-policy and yield pressure; softer inflation can reduce that support.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger U.S. inflation can support the dollar if it raises Fed-policy and yield pressure; softer inflation can reduce that support.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Strong U.S. labor data can support the dollar through Fed-policy and growth channels; weak labor can pressure the dollar if rate expectations fall.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Strong U.S. labor data can support the dollar through Fed-policy and growth channels; weak labor can pressure the dollar if rate expectations fall.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Strong U.S. labor data can support the dollar through Fed-policy and growth channels; weak labor can pressure the dollar if rate expectations fall.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Traders in Financial Futures - Futures Only for EURO FX - CHICAGO MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-05-12: Leveraged Funds long 115,836, short 97,833, net 18,003 (2.17% of open interest), effective for this asset 2.17%.
factors.effect: Leveraged Funds are net long in the related currency futures contract, which is neutral for this FX read. Effective positioning equals 2.17% of open interest. This reflects speculative participation, not a timing signal.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 6,157; effective weekly change 0.74% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Euro / U.S. Dollar. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 37.70%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

assets.name: U.S. Dollar / Japanese Yen
assets.mainConflict: Inflation and labor must be read with Treasury pressure and positioning
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for U.S. Dollar / Japanese Yen: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher relative policy rates usually support USDJPY; lower or dovish policy usually pressures it.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider yield spreads in favor of USDJPY usually support the currency pair; narrowing spreads usually pressure it.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider inflation-adjusted yield advantage usually supports USDJPY; a weaker real-yield spread usually pressures it.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on USDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect USDJPY through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Core PCE is important for policy expectations; hotter PCE usually supports rate/yield pressure and can support the dollar side of USDJPY.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger relative growth can support USDJPY by attracting capital and lifting rate expectations.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer data can support USDJPY if it lifts growth and rate expectations.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect USDJPY through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.

factors.effect: Low direct effect on NZDUSD. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NZDUSD. Watch it only if it spills into rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NZDUSD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NZDUSD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NZDUSD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on NZDUSD. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on NZDUSD. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger U.S. inflation can support USD through rate expectations, which usually pressures NZDUSD; softer inflation can support the pair by reducing USD pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger U.S. inflation can support USD through rate expectations, which usually pressures NZDUSD; softer inflation can support the pair by reducing USD pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger U.S. inflation can support USD through rate expectations, which usually pressures NZDUSD; softer inflation can support the pair by reducing USD pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Strong U.S. labor data can support USD, which usually pressures NZDUSD; weak labor can reduce USD pressure and support the pair.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Strong U.S. labor data can support USD, which usually pressures NZDUSD; weak labor can reduce USD pressure and support the pair.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Primary effect: Strong U.S. labor data can support USD, which usually pressures NZDUSD; weak labor can reduce USD pressure and support the pair.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

assets.name: Euro / British Pound
assets.mainConflict: Inflation and labor must be read with Treasury pressure and positioning
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for Euro / British Pound: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher relative policy rates usually support EURGBP; lower or dovish policy usually pressures it.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider yield spreads in favor of EURGBP usually support the currency pair; narrowing spreads usually pressure it.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider inflation-adjusted yield advantage usually supports EURGBP; a weaker real-yield spread usually pressures it.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EURGBP. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EURGBP. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect EURGBP through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Core PCE is important for policy expectations; hotter PCE usually supports rate/yield pressure and can support the dollar side of EURGBP.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger relative growth can support EURGBP by attracting capital and lifting rate expectations.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer data can support EURGBP if it lifts growth and rate expectations.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect EURGBP through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Tighter conditions can support safe-haven currency demand and pressure high-beta FX linked to EURGBP.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for EURGBP. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Low direct effect on EURGBP. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on EURGBP. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Low direct effect on EURGBP. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS CPI pressure can affect Euro / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS core CPI pressure can affect Euro / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS PPI pressure can affect Euro / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS unemployment rate can affect Euro / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS payroll growth can affect Euro / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS wage pressure can affect Euro / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

assets.name: Australian Dollar / Japanese Yen
assets.mainConflict: Inflation and labor must be read with Treasury pressure and positioning
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for Australian Dollar / Japanese Yen: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher relative policy rates usually support AUDJPY; lower or dovish policy usually pressures it.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider yield spreads in favor of AUDJPY usually support the currency pair; narrowing spreads usually pressure it.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider inflation-adjusted yield advantage usually supports AUDJPY; a weaker real-yield spread usually pressures it.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect AUDJPY through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Core PCE is important for policy expectations; hotter PCE usually supports rate/yield pressure and can support the dollar side of AUDJPY.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger relative growth can support AUDJPY by attracting capital and lifting rate expectations.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Stronger consumer data can support AUDJPY if it lifts growth and rate expectations.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect AUDJPY through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Tighter conditions can support safe-haven currency demand and pressure high-beta FX linked to AUDJPY.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for AUDJPY. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on AUDJPY. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on AUDJPY. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS CPI pressure can affect Australian Dollar / Japanese Yen through macro policy, growth, inflation, and risk channels, but it is not the main direct driver.

assets.name: U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield
assets.mainConflict: Inflation and labor must be read with Treasury pressure and positioning
assets.quick: Official Treasury lane: 2 Yr is 4.09% as of 2026-05-15. Prior observation was 4.00% on 2026-05-14. Yield rose versus the prior Treasury observation.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher policy-rate expectations usually push yield pressure higher for 2Y; easier policy usually lowers rate pressure.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15; previous 4.00% on 2026-05-14
factors.effect: For the U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield, this official yield is the direct input. Higher yields mean stronger rate pressure; lower yields mean weaker rate pressure. This is a public-source rates input, not a trade-timing signal.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: 10-year government bond yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: The 10Y yield is the direct macro-rate input for 2Y; higher readings mean more long-rate pressure.

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: 2Y minus 10Y, 5Y minus 30Y, or comparable curve slope
factors.effect: Primary effect: The curve is a direct regime input for 2Y; steepening can imply changing growth/inflation expectations, while flattening can warn of restrictive policy or growth stress.

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Real yields affect 2Y through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Breakevens affect 2Y through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Primary effect: Core PCE is important for policy expectations; hotter PCE usually supports rate/yield pressure and can support the dollar side of 2Y.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually raises yield pressure for 2Y; weaker growth usually lowers yields and raises slowdown risk.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Consumer demand affects 2Y through growth expectations and policy reaction.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect 2Y through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect 2Y through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for 2Y. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 2Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 2Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 2Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 2Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 2Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Traders in Financial Futures - Futures Only for UST 2Y NOTE - CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE on 2026-05-12: Leveraged Funds long 407,820, short 2,355,649, net -1,947,829 (-40.61% of open interest), effective for this asset 40.61%.
factors.effect: Leveraged Funds are net short in the related Treasury futures contract; after yield inversion this is supportive for U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield. Effective positioning equals 40.61% of open interest. Long Treasury futures usually imply bond-price support and lower-yield pressure.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 86,646; effective weekly change -1.81% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 1.90%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports U.S. 2-Year Treasury Yield rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on 10Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 10Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Traders in Financial Futures - Futures Only for UST 10Y NOTE - CHICAGO BOARD OF TRADE on 2026-05-12: Leveraged Funds long 378,452, short 2,335,394, net -1,956,942 (-33.36% of open interest), effective for this asset 33.36%.
factors.effect: Leveraged Funds are net short in the related Treasury futures contract; after yield inversion this is supportive for U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield. Effective positioning equals 33.36% of open interest. Long Treasury futures usually imply bond-price support and lower-yield pressure.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 46,906; effective weekly change -0.80% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 3.80%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Stronger inflation usually supports higher U.S. rate pressure for U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield because markets expect tighter policy or firmer nominal yields; softer inflation reduces that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: Strong labor data usually supports U.S. 10-Year Treasury Yield rate pressure because it can keep Fed policy firmer; weak labor usually reduces rate pressure but can raise recession concerns.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

assets.name: U.S. 30-Year Treasury Yield
assets.mainConflict: Inflation and labor must be read with Treasury pressure and positioning
assets.quick: Official Treasury lane: 30 Yr is 5.12% as of 2026-05-15. Prior observation was 5.02% on 2026-05-14. Yield rose sharply versus the prior Treasury observation.

factors.name: 30Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 30 Yr par yield: 5.12% on 2026-05-15; previous 5.02% on 2026-05-14
factors.effect: For the U.S. 30-Year Treasury Yield, this official yield is the direct input. Higher yields mean stronger rate pressure; lower yields mean weaker rate pressure. This is a public-source rates input, not a trade-timing signal.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher policy-rate expectations usually push yield pressure higher for 30Y; easier policy usually lowers rate pressure.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: 2-year government bond yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: The 2Y yield is a direct policy-expectations input for 30Y; higher 2Y yield means stronger front-end rate pressure.

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: 10-year government bond yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: The 10Y yield is the direct macro-rate input for 30Y; higher readings mean more long-rate pressure.

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: 2Y minus 10Y, 5Y minus 30Y, or comparable curve slope
factors.effect: Primary effect: The curve is a direct regime input for 30Y; steepening can imply changing growth/inflation expectations, while flattening can warn of restrictive policy or growth stress.

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Real yields affect 30Y through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Breakevens affect 30Y through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Primary effect: Core PCE is important for policy expectations; hotter PCE usually supports rate/yield pressure and can support the dollar side of 30Y.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually raises yield pressure for 30Y; weaker growth usually lowers yields and raises slowdown risk.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Consumer demand affects 30Y through growth expectations and policy reaction.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Credit spreads affect 30Y through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect 30Y through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for 30Y. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on 30Y. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available

factors.effect: Low direct effect on 30Y. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports
