assets.name: High-Yield Credit Spreads
assets.mainConflict: Strong growth can support risk, but hot inflation/labor can keep rates restrictive
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for High-Yield Credit Spreads: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Policy rates affect HY Spread mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Primary effect: The yield curve affects HY Spread through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Real yields affect HY Spread through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Context only: Breakevens affect HY Spread through inflation expectations and real-yield pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: PCE affects HY Spread through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Growth affects HY Spread through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Primary effect: Wider credit spreads usually pressure HY Spread by signaling funding stress and risk-off conditions; tighter spreads usually support risk.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Primary effect: Financial conditions affect HY Spread through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for HY Spread. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on HY Spread. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on HY Spread. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hot inflation can pressure High-Yield Credit Spreads through tighter policy, higher financing costs, and weaker risk appetite; softer inflation usually eases that stress.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.

factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor supports credit through growth, while weak labor can widen stress if recession risk rises. Very strong labor can still pressure credit through higher rates.

factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor supports credit through growth, while weak labor can widen stress if recession risk rises. Very strong labor can still pressure credit through higher rates.

factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor supports credit through growth, while weak labor can widen stress if recession risk rises. Very strong labor can still pressure credit through higher rates.

factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Context only: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Product supplied / demand
factors.derived: EIA product supplied for gasoline, distillates, and total products
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Context only: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Natural gas storage
factors.derived: Weekly gas storage change and storage vs 5-year average
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on FCI. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on FCI. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hot inflation can pressure Financial Conditions Fundamental Pressure through tighter policy, higher financing costs, and weaker risk appetite; softer inflation usually eases that stress.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS core CPI pressure
factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hot inflation can pressure Financial Conditions Fundamental Pressure through tighter policy, higher financing costs, and weaker risk appetite; softer inflation usually eases that stress.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS PPI pressure
factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hot inflation can pressure Financial Conditions Fundamental Pressure through tighter policy, higher financing costs, and weaker risk appetite; softer inflation usually eases that stress.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor supports credit through growth, while weak labor can widen stress if recession risk rises. Very strong labor can still pressure credit through higher rates.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor supports credit through growth, while weak labor can widen stress if recession risk rises. Very strong labor can still pressure credit through higher rates.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS wage pressure
factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor supports credit through growth, while weak labor can widen stress if recession risk rises. Very strong labor can still pressure credit through higher rates.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for Gold Futures / XAUUSD: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through rates, discount-rate pressure, or funding conditions.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising real yields usually pressure gold because gold pays no yield; falling real yields usually support gold.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising breakevens can support gold if real yields do not rise faster; falling breakevens weaken the inflation-hedge case.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Hotter PCE can help gold as inflation pressure only if real yields do not rise; Fed-driven real-yield pressure can outweigh it.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Context only: Growth affects Gold through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gold. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through broad macro pressure.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Wider credit spreads can support gold through safe-haven demand, but only if real-yield pressure does not offset it.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for GOLD - COMMODITY EXCHANGE INC. on 2026-05-12: Managed Money long 127,242, short 29,227, net 98,015 (26.03% of open interest), effective for this asset 26.03%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 26.03% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change 3,761; effective weekly change 1.00% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for Gold Futures / XAUUSD. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 52.80%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -20,202 (-5.37% of open interest); recent percentile 84.90%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Gold Futures / XAUUSD, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 26.03%; commercial/hedger positioning -5.37%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Gold Futures / XAUUSD. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels. | U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: EIA petroleum balance does not normally drive Gold Futures / XAUUSD. It matters indirectly if energy shocks change inflation expectations, real-yield pressure, or risk sentiment.

factors.effect: Low direct effect: natural gas storage does not normally drive Gold Futures / XAUUSD; it only matters through broad inflation or energy-stress channels.

factors.effect: Secondary effect: Strong labor usually pressures Gold Futures / XAUUSD through higher rate/real-yield expectations; weak labor can support it if yields fall or safe-haven demand rises.

assets.name: Brent Crude Oil
assets.coverage: COT: Managed Money / 2026-05-12 | EIA energy fundamentals | BLS inflation/labor
assets.mainConflict: Energy fundamentals must be read with COT and macro pressure
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for Brent Crude Oil: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect Brent Oil mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect Brent Oil indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects Brent Oil through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Brent Oil through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support Brent Oil as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects Brent Oil through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports Brent Oil by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support Brent Oil if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect Brent Oil through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect Brent Oil through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Brent Oil. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Brent Oil. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Brent Oil. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Brent Oil. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for BRENT LAST DAY - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-05-12: Managed Money long 19,082, short 4,019, net 15,063 (6.10% of open interest), effective for this asset 6.10%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 6.10% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 73.60%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme

factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For Brent Crude Oil, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 6.10%; commercial/hedger positioning -11.92%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for Brent Crude Oil. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.derived: Lower 48 working natural gas in underground storage (png.nw2_epg0_swo_r48_bcf.w) latest 2,290 bcf on 2026-05-08; previous 2,205 bcf on 2026-05-01. Weekly change: 85.0 bcf. Storage versus five-year average: 6.5%.
factors.effect: Direct effect: lower-than-normal gas storage supports natural gas because the supply cushion is tighter; above-normal storage pressures natural gas.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA crude inventories
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: crude inventories do not normally drive natural gas. They matter only through broad energy/inflation context.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA gasoline inventories
factors.derived: U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: gasoline stocks do not normally drive natural gas except through broad energy-complex context.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA distillate inventories
factors.derived: U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: distillate tightness can matter during winter heating stress, but gas storage is the primary input.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

assets.name: RBOB Gasoline
assets.coverage: COT: Managed Money / 2026-05-12 | EIA energy fundamentals | BLS inflation/labor
assets.mainConflict: Energy fundamentals must be read with COT and macro pressure
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for RBOB Gasoline: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect Gasoline mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect Gasoline indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects Gasoline through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Context only: Real yields affect Gasoline through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Rising breakevens can support Gasoline as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects Gasoline through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Stronger growth usually supports Gasoline by improving demand expectations; weaker growth pressures demand.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger demand can support Gasoline if it implies more fuel and transportation use; weak demand pressures the energy read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect Gasoline through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect Gasoline through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for Gasoline. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Primary effect: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Primary effect: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Gasoline. Watch it only if it spills into food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Low direct effect on Gasoline. Watch it only if it spills into energy inflation, growth expectations, or broad risk sentiment.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Normally no direct effect on Gasoline. Do not score it for this asset unless it creates an unusual shock through food inflation, supply shocks, or policy/risk spillover.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: Disaggregated - Futures Only for GASOLINE RBOB - NEW YORK MERCANTILE EXCHANGE on 2026-05-12: Managed Money long 68,169, short 5,234, net 62,935 (19.20% of open interest), effective for this asset 19.20%.
factors.effect: Managed Money are net long in the related commodity futures contract, which is supportive for this commodity. Effective positioning equals 19.20% of open interest. This shows trend participation, but extremes can become unwind risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly net change -2,768; effective weekly change -0.84% of open interest.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether speculative participation is getting stronger or weaker for RBOB Gasoline. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the positioning read; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning percentile over recent lookback: 77.40%.
factors.effect: Spec positioning is not at a recent extreme, so crowding risk is not a major signal yet.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/Merchant/Processor/User net -79,647 (-24.30% of open interest); recent percentile 15.10%.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic buy/sell signal. For RBOB Gasoline, extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context, while extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Spec effective positioning 19.20%; commercial/hedger positioning -24.30%.
factors.effect: COT conflict is present for RBOB Gasoline. When specs and commercials are strongly opposed, the directional read may still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: EIA gasoline inventories
factors.derived: U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Direct effect: gasoline inventory draws usually support gasoline because product supply tightens; builds usually pressure gasoline.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA crude inventories
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: crude draws can support input-cost and energy-complex context, but gasoline stocks are the more direct input.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing, Oklahoma crude oil stocks (W_EPC0_SAX_YCUOK_MBBL) latest 27,422 thousand barrels on 2026-05-08; previous 29,124 thousand barrels on 2026-05-01. Weekly change: -1,702 thousand barrels.
factors.effect: Low direct effect: Cushing is crude-specific; it matters for gasoline only through crude-market context.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA distillate inventories
factors.derived: U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: distillate stocks help read overall refined-product tightness but are not the main gasoline driver.

factors.effect: Contextual effect: natural gas storage can affect winter heating-energy sentiment, but distillate inventories remain the direct input.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS inflation does not directly set Heating Oil / Distillates supply/demand, but it affects macro pressure, policy expectations, and broad commodity inflation sentiment.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.derived: PPI final demand (WPUFD4) latest 156.88 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 5.99%; 3-month annualized pace 13.13%.

assets.name: Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket
assets.coverage: public-source prototype | EIA energy fundamentals | USDA agriculture fundamentals | BLS inflation/labor
assets.mainConflict: Macro evidence mixed
assets.quick: Public-source fundamental pressure read for Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket: open to see official/public-source inputs across macro, policy, positioning, rates, credit, energy, agriculture, and liquidity where relevant.

factors.name: Policy rate
factors.derived: Current central bank target rate or policy band
factors.effect: Context only: Policy rates affect CMD-FUND mainly through currency, financing-cost, and demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Rate differentials
factors.derived: Relevant yield spread between countries or curves, usually 2Y and 10Y comparisons
factors.effect: Context only: Rate differentials affect CMD-FUND indirectly through currency pressure, capital flows, and discount-rate expectations. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Real yield differentials
factors.derived: Inflation-adjusted yield spread, often 10Y real yield vs peer real yield
factors.effect: Context only: Real-yield differentials matter through inflation-adjusted return; rising real yields usually pressure gold and risk assets but can support the dollar. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: 2Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 2 Yr par yield: 4.09% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 2Y yields usually mean stronger front-end policy pressure; lower 2Y yields reduce that pressure. The effect is direct for rate assets and indirect for other assets through policy, dollar, and risk channels.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: 10Y yield
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10 Yr par yield: 4.59% on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Higher 10Y yields usually increase long-rate pressure and can pressure risk assets and gold; lower 10Y yields usually ease that pressure.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Yield curve
factors.derived: Official Treasury 10Y minus 2Y curve spread: 0.500 percentage points on 2026-05-15
factors.effect: Context only: The yield curve affects CMD-FUND through growth expectations, policy tightness, and recession-risk signaling. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: U.S. Treasury official Daily Treasury Par Yield Curve Rates

factors.name: Real yields
factors.derived: 10Y TIPS real yield trend and level
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Real yields affect CMD-FUND through inflation-adjusted return and discount-rate pressure.

factors.name: Breakeven inflation
factors.derived: 5Y and 10Y breakeven inflation
factors.effect: Primary effect: Rising breakevens can support CMD-FUND as inflation pressure, but demand and supply fundamentals still dominate.

factors.name: PCE pressure
factors.derived: PCE and Core PCE inflation readings
factors.effect: Context only: PCE affects CMD-FUND through the Fed-policy and inflation-expectations channel. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: GDP / growth trend
factors.derived: GDP growth, nowcast, or growth surprise vs prior/forecast
factors.effect: Primary effect: Growth affects CMD-FUND through demand, capital flows, and policy expectations.

factors.name: Retail sales / consumer demand
factors.derived: Retail sales headline/control group and surprise
factors.effect: Context only: Consumer demand affects CMD-FUND through growth expectations and policy reaction. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Credit spreads
factors.derived: High-yield and investment-grade spreads vs history
factors.effect: Context only: Credit spreads affect CMD-FUND through risk appetite and funding-stress conditions. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Financial conditions
factors.derived: Financial conditions index or composite of rates, dollar, equities, and credit
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Financial conditions affect CMD-FUND through liquidity, credit availability, and risk appetite.
factors.source: FRED / Federal Reserve public-source candidate with series rights check

factors.name: Open interest
factors.derived: Futures open interest trend and change
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Open interest helps show conviction behind futures positioning for CMD-FUND. Rising OI with a supportive COT read adds weight; falling OI weakens confirmation.

factors.name: Crude inventories
factors.derived: Weekly U.S. commercial crude oil stock change
factors.effect: Primary effect: Crude inventory draws usually support oil-linked assets by signaling tighter supply; builds usually pressure them.

factors.name: Cushing inventories
factors.derived: Cushing crude stock level and weekly change
factors.effect: Context only: Cushing stocks are especially important for WTI-linked reads; falling Cushing inventories usually tighten WTI conditions, while builds pressure WTI. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Product inventories
factors.derived: Gasoline and distillate stock changes
factors.effect: Context only: Rising gasoline/distillate stocks can pressure fuel and crude demand reads; draws usually support the energy complex. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Refinery utilization
factors.derived: Percent of refinery capacity utilized
factors.effect: Context only: Higher refinery utilization usually supports crude demand; lower utilization can weaken crude demand even if headline inventories look supportive. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: U.S. production
factors.derived: U.S. crude or natural gas production estimate
factors.effect: Context only: Rising U.S. production adds supply and can pressure oil/gas; falling production can tighten balance and support prices fundamentally. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Imports / exports
factors.derived: Petroleum or commodity trade flow changes
factors.effect: Context only: Trade flows explain inventory pressure. Higher exports can tighten domestic balances; higher imports can loosen them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: OPEC policy
factors.derived: OPEC official production targets, cuts, increases, compliance notes, and public monthly releases
factors.effect: Secondary effect: OPEC cuts usually support oil by reducing supply; production increases or weak compliance usually pressure the oil balance.

factors.name: Weather HDD/CDD
factors.derived: Heating-degree days, cooling-degree days, and public weather/forecast deviations where available
factors.effect: Context only: Hot/cold weather raises cooling/heating demand and usually supports natural gas; mild weather usually pressures demand. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: LNG exports / power burn
factors.derived: LNG export flows and gas used for electricity generation
factors.effect: Context only: Higher LNG exports or power burn supports natural gas demand; weaker flows reduce demand pressure. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: WASDE ending stocks
factors.derived: USDA WASDE ending stocks for wheat, corn, soybeans, cotton
factors.effect: Primary effect: Lower ending stocks usually support the crop market because supply cushion is thinner; higher stocks usually pressure it.

factors.name: Stock/use ratio
factors.derived: Ending stocks divided by total use
factors.effect: Context only: A lower stock/use ratio means tighter supply relative to demand and usually supports the crop; a higher ratio usually pressures it. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Crop progress / condition
factors.derived: Planting, emergence, harvest progress, and good/excellent crop ratings
factors.effect: Context only: Poor crop condition or delayed progress usually supports crop prices; strong conditions and smooth progress usually pressure them. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Export sales
factors.derived: Weekly export sales and shipments
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger export sales support demand for the crop; weak sales pressure the demand read. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Weather / drought
factors.derived: NOAA/NWS weather, drought monitor, rainfall, heat/freeze risk, and crop-region conditions where available
factors.effect: Secondary effect: Adverse weather or drought usually supports crop prices by threatening supply; favorable weather usually pressures the supply-risk premium.

factors.name: Global supply competitors
factors.derived: Brazil/Argentina/Black Sea/EU/Australia supply where relevant
factors.effect: Context only: Larger competing supply can pressure the crop; supply problems in major exporters usually support it. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: Ethanol / biofuel linkage
factors.derived: EIA/USDA ethanol production, corn use for ethanol, and biofuel-demand data
factors.effect: Context only: Stronger biofuel demand supports corn/soy oil demand; weaker biofuel demand reduces support. Use it to confirm or challenge the read, not as the main driver.

factors.name: COT spec direction
factors.derived: CFTC COT net speculative positioning for the mapped futures contract; live values appear after Refresh CFTC COT Lane runs.
factors.effect: Spec direction shows trend participation. Net long speculative exposure can support the asset; net short exposure can pressure it, with inversion applied for Treasury yield assets and USD-base FX pairs.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec weekly change
factors.derived: Weekly change in speculative net exposure from the CFTC COT report.
factors.effect: Weekly change shows whether specs are adding to or reducing the directional positioning read. Adding exposure in the supportive direction strengthens the signal; reducing exposure weakens it.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT crowding risk
factors.derived: Recent percentile/range position of speculative net exposure where available.
factors.effect: Crowding risk separates participation from fragility. Stretched long positioning can create unwind risk; stretched short positioning can create short-covering risk.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT commercial / hedger extreme
factors.derived: Producer/merchant/processor/user positioning for commodity futures when available.
factors.effect: Commercial/hedger positioning is not an automatic signal. Extreme commercial long exposure can suggest value/support context; extreme commercial short exposure can warn that speculative long exposure may be crowded.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: COT spec-commercial conflict
factors.derived: Comparison of speculative positioning against commercial/hedger positioning where available.
factors.effect: Conflict means specs and commercials are meaningfully opposed. The trend participation read can still work, but reversal or unwind risk deserves more attention.
factors.source: CFTC Commitments of Traders public reports

factors.name: EIA petroleum inventory balance
factors.derived: U.S. commercial crude oil stocks excluding SPR (W_EPC0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest 499,740 thousand barrels on 2016-09-30; previous 502,716 thousand barrels on 2016-09-23. Weekly change: -2,976 thousand barrels. | U.S. motor gasoline stocks (W_EPM0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None. | U.S. distillate fuel oil stocks (W_EPD0_SAX_NUS_MBBL) latest missing thousand barrels on None; previous missing thousand barrels on None.
factors.effect: Secondary effect: EIA petroleum draws support the broad commodity basket through energy tightness and inflation pressure; builds reduce that support.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: EIA natural gas storage
factors.derived: Lower 48 working natural gas in underground storage (png.nw2_epg0_swo_r48_bcf.w) latest 2,290 bcf on 2026-05-08; previous 2,205 bcf on 2026-05-01. Weekly change: 85.0 bcf. Storage versus five-year average: 6.5%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: natural gas storage affects the energy component of a broad commodity read, but it is not the whole basket.
factors.source: U.S. Energy Information Administration open data/API

factors.name: USDA crop condition
factors.derived: USDA/NASS crop-condition observations missing.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: Crop stress supports the broad commodity basket through food/inflation pressure; strong crop conditions reduce that support.
factors.source: USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service Quick Stats API

factors.name: BLS CPI pressure
factors.derived: CPI all items (CUSR0000SA0) latest 332.41 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 3.95%; 3-month annualized pace 7.32%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS inflation does not directly set Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket supply/demand, but it affects macro pressure, policy expectations, and broad commodity inflation sentiment.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.derived: Core CPI less food and energy (CUSR0000SA0L1E) latest 335.42 on 2026-04-01; year-over-year change 2.99%; 3-month annualized pace 3.20%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS inflation does not directly set Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket supply/demand, but it affects macro pressure, policy expectations, and broad commodity inflation sentiment.

factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS inflation does not directly set Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket supply/demand, but it affects macro pressure, policy expectations, and broad commodity inflation sentiment.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS unemployment rate
factors.derived: Unemployment rate (LNS14000000) latest 4.30 percent on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.00 percent; year-over-year change 2.38%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS labor data does not directly drive Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket supply, but it affects growth demand, Fed expectations, dollar pressure, and risk appetite.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.name: BLS payroll growth
factors.derived: Total nonfarm payroll employment (CES0000000001) latest 158,736.00 thousands of jobs on 2026-04-01; one-month change 115.00 thousands of jobs; year-over-year change 0.16%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS labor data does not directly drive Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket supply, but it affects growth demand, Fed expectations, dollar pressure, and risk appetite.
factors.source: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics public API

factors.derived: Average hourly earnings private employees (CES0500000003) latest 37.41 dollars/hour on 2026-04-01; one-month change 0.06 dollars/hour; year-over-year change 3.57%.
factors.effect: Contextual effect: BLS labor data does not directly drive Broad Commodity Fundamental Basket supply, but it affects growth demand, Fed expectations, dollar pressure, and risk appetite.

assets.name: Corn Futures
